my_thoughts: The internal authority works: definite objective inner standards, psychological landmarks > philosophical ones. The student is seeking source of truth (epistemological inquiry), which shows they're seeking external authority. They'll likely ask about laziness next. Hypothesis: authority questions trigger moral judgment questions—testable by tracking whether authority questions lead to moral judgment inquiries.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student asks epistemological question ('Who assesses psychological values?')—seeking source of truth via epistemological inquiry and authority seeking. They're seeking external authority (who assesses). The comparison_contrast mechanism establishes: internal authority (definite objective inner standards), psychological landmarks > philosophical ones (philosophical = words/rhetoric, psychological = no mistake for oneself). Pattern: authority question → internal authority → [next: laziness question]. Medium confidence because authority questions typically trigger clarification requests.
visible_response:
There are definite objective signs by which one can judge—definite inner standards.
At a certain point they may become objective.
As I said, we do not look for
philosophical landmarks, we want psychological landmarks. It is very important to
understand this.
Philosophical conclusions may be just words, rhetoric, but about
psychological landmarks there can be no mistake—for oneself.
